curriculumNotes: Dosage Math: Only topics without blocked external prerequisites in this slice: basic-math-fractions-decimals-percentages, then systems-of-measurement. oral-medication-dosage-calculations is deferred because blueprint lists rights-of-medication-administration (Pharmacology) as a prerequisite.

Anatomy & Physiology: organization-of-the-human-body normally follows Medical Terminology (body-directions-and-planes, body-cavities-and-regions). Lesson 1 embeds a compact orientation bridge so this batch can stand alone without violating learner sequencing for directional language.
Downstream A&P (e.g., cell-structure-and-function) requires organic-molecules-macromolecules per blueprint; not included in this domain-only batch.

topics.topicName: Basic Math Review: Fractions, Decimals, and Percentages

lessons.title: Fractions and ratios: what they mean for doses

structuredContent.overview: Nursing math starts with part-whole relationships. Fractions describe portions of tablets and liquid volumes; ratios link related amounts such as mg per mL.
structuredContent.mentalModel: A half tablet is 1/2 of a whole. Two halves make one whole because 2 × 1/2 = 1. Ratios stay balanced until you intentionally scale both sides.
stepByStep: Multiply fractions: multiply numerators; multiply denominators; simplify.
Divide fractions: invert the divisor and multiply.
Add/subtract fractions: common denominator, then add/subtract numerators.
Improper fractions can become mixed numbers when that matches how orders are written (e.g., 5/2 tablets = 2 1/2 tablets).
commonMistakes: Adding numerators without a common denominator.
Canceling across addition (reliable canceling is for multiplication).
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Fraction sense prevents wrong setups before unit conversion.

structuredContent.overview: Decimals are base-10 fractions. Round at the end, to a precision allowed by the device and your program policy.
structuredContent.mentalModel: Each decimal place to the right is ten times smaller. Multiplying by 10 moves the decimal right; dividing by 10 moves it left.
stepByStep: Add/subtract: align decimal points; use placeholder zeros.
Multiply: multiply as integers; total decimal places from both factors.
Divide: shift decimals equally in divisor and dividend until divisor is whole.
Round last; syringe increments and tablet split rules decide sensible precision.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: mL dosing on syringes depends on decimal discipline; ten-fold errors are a known failure mode.
structuredContent.workedExample: 2.5 mL + 0.75 mL = 3.25 mL; measurability depends on syringe graduations.

structuredContent.overview: Percent means per 100. Proportions state equal ratios and support dimensional analysis later.
structuredContent.mentalModel: 25% = 0.25. 'Of' often means multiply. If a/b = c/d, cross-products align when the proportion is valid.
stepByStep: Percent of a number: convert percent, then multiply.
What percent is a of b?: (a / b) × 100%.
Solve unknowns in proportions with cross-multiplication; keep units consistent.
commonMistakes: Using % directly in a calculation that needs decimal form.
Unlike units on the same side of a proportion.
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Percent concentrations appear in IV fluids; proportional thinking transfers to dose setup.

topics.topicName: Systems of Measurement: Metric, Household, and Apothecary

lessons.title: Metric base units and prefixes used in nursing

structuredContent.overview: Metric mass uses grams; volume uses liters; length uses meters. Prefixes move by powers of 10.
keyTerms: kilo (k): multiply the base unit by 1000
centi (c): divide the base unit by 100
milli (m): divide the base unit by 1000
micro (mcg): divide the gram by 1,000,000 (say micrograms aloud)
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: mg vs mcg confusion is high-risk; verbalize units.
commonMistakes: assuming mL differs from cc for liquid volume

structuredContent.overview: Patients may reference tsp/Tbsp/oz at home; some legacy orders mention grains. Convert to metric for acute care documentation and teaching.
keyTerms: Teaching approximations (confirm with your program): 1 tsp ~ 5 mL; 1 Tbsp ~ 15 mL; 1 fluid oz ~ 30 mL
gr historically abbreviates grain; g abbreviates gram
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Kitchen spoons vary; metric dosing tools are standardized in hospitals.
commonMistakes: mixing weight ounces with fluid ounces

questions.stem: Which metric unit family is used for medication mass on most labels?
questions.rationale: Solid dosing mass uses gram prefixes; liquids use volume.

whyIncorrect.D: Teaspoons are volume and not mass.

questions.rationale: Milli- to micro- is three decimal steps: ×1000 mcg per mg.

questions.stem: Which equality is correct for volume in clinical dosing?
questions.rationale: A cubic centimeter is the same volume as a milliliter.

questions.stem: The prefix centi- (c) means which change from the base unit?
The prefix milli- (m) means which change from the base unit?
The prefix kilo- (k) means which change from the base unit?
questions.rationale: Kilo- is one thousand times the base unit.
1 g = 1000 mg, larger than the other values listed.

questions.stem: Many programs teach 1 teaspoon (tsp) as approximately:
questions.rationale: Common teaching conversion (confirm with your program).

whyIncorrect.B: Often used for 1 Tbsp in teaching charts.
whyIncorrect.C: Closer to 1 fluid oz in many charts.

questions.stem: Many programs teach 1 tablespoon (Tbsp) as approximately:
questions.rationale: Common teaching conversion (confirm with your program).

whyIncorrect.C: Closer to 1 fluid oz in many charts.

questions.stem: Why are metric syringes preferred over kitchen spoons for medication measurement?

options.A: Kitchen spoons vary; metric tools are standardized
options.B: Kitchen spoons are always exactly 5 mL

questions.stem: The meter (m) is the base metric unit for:
The gram (g) is a base metric unit for:
The liter (L) is a base metric unit for:
Which pair expresses the same mass?
In historic/legacy notation familiarity, gr commonly abbreviates:
questions.rationale: gr is grain; do not confuse with g for gram.

questions.stem: How many mcg are in 0.05 mg?

questions.rationale: Multiply mg by 1000 to get mcg.

questions.stem: Which is the best metric-only expression of a volume for liquid medication in acute care?
questions.rationale: Acute care standardizes metric volume dosing.

whyIncorrect.B: May appear in home context; not preferred alone in acute care.

questions.stem: Which statement about mg and mcg is true?

options.C: mg and mcg are volume units
options.D: mg and mcg cannot be compared

questions.rationale: Milligram is three orders of magnitude larger than microgram.

whyIncorrect.D: They can be compared by conversion.

questions.rationale: One milligram is one thousandth of a gram.

topics.prerequisiteHandling: Embedded prerequisite bridge for blueprint topics body-directions-and-planes and body-cavities-and-regions inside Lesson 1.

lessons.title: Orientation: position, directions, planes, cavities, levels of organization

structuredContent.overview: Shared anatomical language prevents ambiguity in documentation and study.

orientationPrerequisiteBridge.anatomicalPosition: Upright, feet slightly apart, palms forward, eyes forward.
planes: Sagittal: left vs right (midsagittal on midline)
cavitiesAndRegions: Ventral: thoracic and abdominopelvic; quadrants RUQ/LUQ/RLQ/LLQ for localization

levelsOfOrganization: Chemical → Cell → Tissue → Organ → Organ system → Organism
structuredContent.mentalModel: Smaller units combine into larger working teams.
commonMistakes: Patient left vs observer left confusion
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: Directional and quadrant language appears in reports and handoffs at a foundational documentation level.

lessons.title: Organ systems survey: names and one-line roles

structuredContent.overview: Pre-nursing goal: correct names plus a single plain purpose per system—no advanced clinical reasoning.

systemsTable.function: Filtration; electrolyte/water regulation; waste removal in urine

commonMistakes: forgetting absorption/elimination roles of digestive system beyond digestion
structuredContent.clinicalRelevanceLight: System names organize all later A&P and assessment learning.

questions.stem: In anatomical position, the palms face:
questions.rationale: Palms forward is part of the standard anatomical position.

whyIncorrect.C: Describes direction to feet, not palm orientation.
whyIncorrect.D: Medial is midline-related, not default palm direction.

questions.stem: The nose is ______ to the ears.
questions.rationale: The nose is closer to the midline than the ears.

whyIncorrect.B: Ears are lateral to the nose.
whyIncorrect.C: Not the best term for this relationship.
whyIncorrect.D: Not the best term for this relationship.

questions.stem: The ankle is ______ to the knee.
questions.rationale: Distal is farther from the trunk along the limb.

whyIncorrect.C: Superior is headward; not the primary limb-axis term.

questions.stem: A sagittal plane divides the body into:

whyIncorrect.D: Limb directions, not a whole-body plane pair.

questions.stem: A frontal (coronal) plane divides the body into:
questions.rationale: Frontal plane separates front from back.

whyIncorrect.D: Not the definition of a frontal plane.

questions.stem: A transverse (horizontal) plane divides the body into:
questions.rationale: Transverse cuts separate top from bottom sections.
Skull houses the brain in the cranial cavity.

questions.stem: The spinal cord is protected within the:
The right lower quadrant (RLQ) is which abdominal area?
Which level of organization comes immediately after tissue?
Which is the smallest level listed?
questions.rationale: Cells are building blocks of tissues.

questions.stem: Which system primarily performs gas exchange between air and blood?
Which system transports nutrients and gases in blood?
Which system coordinates slower, widespread regulation using hormones?
The brain and spinal cord are central components of the:
The primary filtration organs of the urinary system are the:
questions.rationale: Kidneys filter blood to form urine.

questions.stem: Which term means toward the front of the body?
Which term means toward the back?

whyIncorrect.C: Closer to trunk on a limb.
whyIncorrect.D: Farther from trunk on a limb.

questions.stem: Skin, hair, and nails belong to the:

whyIncorrect.C: Immune is broader than surface accessories alone.

questions.stem: The midsagittal plane is a sagittal plane that:
The elbow is ______ to the shoulder.
questions.rationale: Elbow is farther down the upper limb from the shoulder.

whyIncorrect.C: Not the primary limb-axis term here.
whyIncorrect.D: Midline term, not primary limb-axis here.

questions.stem: The heart is located in which cavity at survey level?
questions.rationale: Thoracic cavity contains heart and lungs.

questions.stem: Which organ system includes bones and joints at survey level?
Which organ system is primarily responsible for movement and posture?

whyIncorrect.B: Provides leverage but movement is muscular.

questions.stem: Which level comes immediately before the organism in the standard ladder?
questions.rationale: Organ systems cooperate as the whole organism.

questions.stem: Which cavity surrounds the lungs at survey level?
questions.rationale: Each lung has a pleural cavity; both are thoracic.

questions.stem: Which directional pair is opposite: superficial vs ______.
questions.rationale: Superficial and deep describe nearness to the surface.

topicsDifficultToGenerateUnderStrictSequ: Dosage: oral-medication-dosage-calculations and downstream (IV flow, weight-based, safe range) blocked until Pharmacology rights-of-medication-administration / adverse effects prerequisites exist in the same learner path.

A&P: all topics after organization-of-the-human-body blocked by blueprint prerequisites outside this batch (e.g., organic-molecules-macromolecules for cell-structure-and-function; medical terminology topics for several systems).
finalReport.rulesConfirmation: Depth scaled by readinessWeight (critical dosage math = 3 applied lessons; high dosage = 2 recall lessons; medium A&P = 2 shorter lessons). cognitiveLevel matched (early-application math with steps/examples; recall topics with identification/definitions). Question counts meet each topic targetQuestionCountMin. No SATA; single-focus stems; each item has rationale and why distractors fail.
